variations.name: Main Line

annotation.moveRationale: Bc4 develops the bishop actively, targeting the vulnerable f7/f2 square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
d6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nb5 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
b4 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
e4 impacts the center, shaping the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
axb5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nc3 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
g3 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nd2 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
g6 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nf3 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
e4 impacts the center, shaping the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
d6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nc3 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
The most common move. g6 is more flexible.

Nge2 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
d6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Kxf1 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Bxf1 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.

f4 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nf3 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.

Bg2 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.

Kg2 improves the position in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
g3 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Bg7 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Kxf1 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Bxf1 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
e4 impacts the center, shaping the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
d6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nf3 develops a piece to an active square in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Bxa6 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Nc3 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
Black delays the recapture of a6 and prioritizes development instead. g6 simply intends to develop the bishop to g7, from where it will control the a1-h8 diagonal.
The standard Benko Gambit line. Black doesn't have to recapture immediately, since the pawn currently has three attackers.
f3 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
e3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Benko Gambit Accepted.
b6 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Benko Gambit Accepted.

The standard Benko Gambit line. White has three common options available:

*/5.
White accepts the initial pawn. The "real" gambit now follows with 4.
Black offers White the b pawn. Declining comes with disadvantages so White usually accepts it, at least for now.
White keeps their central pawn and takes more space in the centre. White's pawn dug in on d5 cramps Black's position.
Black attacks White's central d-pawn from the flank. The c-pawn is not free: if 3.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

variations.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 b5 4. cxb5 a6
